scenes.narration: Do you know why an AI Agent guesses instead of saying “I don't know”?
scenes.headline: Do you know why an AI Agent guesses instead of saying I don't know?

scenes.narration: OpenAI research gives a clear answer: standard training and evaluation often reward a lucky guess more than an honest expression of uncertainty.
scenes.headline: OpenAI: accuracy can reward AI Agent guessing

scenes.narration: That incentive can turn a plausible sentence into a wrong decision, a false citation, or an unsafe action. You need a way to separate what the AI Agent knows, infers, and cannot verify.
scenes.headline: A fluent AI Agent can still be wrong

scenes.narration: This video builds an AI Agent Uncertainty and Fact-Check Checklist. It helps you test answerability, set an evidence bar, and decide whether to answer, verify, clarify, or abstain.
scenes.headline: Use an AI Agent fact-check checklist

scenes.narration: The core idea is simple: do not ask only whether an answer sounds right. Ask whether the question is answerable, which claims carry risk, and what evidence would change the action.
scenes.headline: Confidence must earn the action

scenes.narration: This video is packed with practical, high-value insights to help you get better at using AI. It's a longer one, so follow Tiny Agent and save it so you don't lose it.
scenes.headline: Practical, high-value AI Agent methods

scenes.narration: Start with the incentive. This chapter shows why a fluent AI Agent can be rewarded for guessing even when it lacks the fact.
scenes.headline: Chapter 2: Guessing Incentives
scenes.coreLabel: See why accuracy can reward the wrong behavior

scenes.narration: During pretraining, a language model learns patterns from large collections of text. It sees fluent statements, but those statements do not arrive with a reliable true-or-false label attached to every claim.
scenes.headline: Pretraining sees fluent statements
scenes.coreLabel: No truth label on every claim

scenes.narration: Frequent structured facts are easier to learn. Rare arbitrary facts, such as an obscure birthday or document title, may have no useful pattern that lets the model reconstruct the missing truth.
scenes.headline: Rare facts have weak patterns

scenes.narration: Post-training should reduce these errors, but many evaluations still score only exact accuracy. A correct guess earns a point, while “I don't know” receives nothing.
scenes.headline: Accuracy-only scoring rewards guesses
scenes.coreLabel: Right earns credit · Unknown earns none

scenes.narration: If a model guesses a birthday, it has a one-in-three-hundred-and-sixty-five chance of being right. Abstaining guarantees no accuracy point, even though it avoids a confident falsehood.
scenes.headline: A lucky guess can hide a falsehood

scenes.narration: OpenAI's SimpleQA example makes the trade-off visible. One model reached twenty-two percent accuracy with twenty-six percent errors and fifty-two percent abstentions; another reached twenty-four percent accuracy but seventy-five percent errors and almost never abstained.
scenes.headline: Similar accuracy can hide very different errors

scenes.narration: So accuracy alone hides the cost of failure. A reliable AI Agent score should penalize confident errors more than honest uncertainty and reward appropriate clarification or abstention.
scenes.headline: Price confident errors above uncertainty

scenes.narration: Chapter recap. First, fluent generation does not attach a truth label to every claim.
scenes.headline: 1. Fluency does not prove truth

scenes.narration: Second, accuracy-only scoring rewards lucky guesses.
scenes.headline: 2. Accuracy can reward luck

scenes.narration: Third, reliable evaluation must price confident errors above uncertainty.
scenes.headline: 3. Error cost belongs in the score

scenes.narration: Before checking an answer, check the question. This chapter helps the AI Agent decide whether a reliable answer is possible with the available information.
scenes.headline: Chapter 3: Answerability
scenes.coreLabel: Decide whether a reliable answer is possible

scenes.narration: Ask four things: is the request specific, is the required information available, is the fact stable enough to answer from memory, and can the result be checked before action?
scenes.headline: Test four answerability conditions

scenes.narration: A missing date, unclear entity, or ambiguous term is not a reasoning challenge. It is a clarification problem, and the AI Agent should ask for the detail that changes the answer.
scenes.headline: Missing context needs clarification

scenes.narration: Current prices, laws, schedules, account state, and private records are answerable only through live or authorized evidence. Without that access, the correct state is unknown.
scenes.headline: Live facts require live evidence

scenes.narration: Some questions are inherently underdetermined. If several answers fit the prompt, the Agent should state the ambiguity and offer the smallest useful choice instead of inventing hidden intent.
scenes.headline: Ambiguity is not hidden intent

scenes.narration: For a low-risk brainstorming prompt, an approximate answer may be useful. For a factual claim that will drive money, health, access, or publication, the evidence bar must be much higher.
scenes.headline: Raise the evidence bar with risk

scenes.narration: Abstention is not a failure when the requested truth cannot be determined. It is a controlled result that protects the user and tells the system what information or tool is missing.
scenes.headline: A justified unknown is a valid result

scenes.narration: Chapter recap. First, test specificity, availability, freshness, and verifiability.
scenes.headline: 1. Test answerability

scenes.narration: Second, turn missing context into a clarification request.
scenes.headline: 2. Ask for missing context

scenes.narration: Third, treat a justified unknown as a valid controlled outcome.
scenes.headline: 3. Accept a justified unknown

scenes.narration: Once the question is answerable, make uncertainty visible. This chapter turns one polished response into facts, inferences, and unknowns the viewer can inspect.
scenes.headline: Chapter 4: Express Uncertainty
scenes.coreLabel: Make facts, inferences, and unknowns visible

scenes.narration: Mark direct facts as facts only when the Agent has supporting evidence. Mark conclusions as inferences when they combine evidence with assumptions or judgment.
scenes.headline: Separate facts from inference

scenes.narration: Use explicit language such as “confirmed,” “supported but incomplete,” “inferred,” or “unknown.” Avoid vague hedges that sound cautious without telling the user what is missing.
scenes.headline: Use explicit evidence states

scenes.narration: Confidence should follow evidence coverage, not the smoothness of the prose. A beautiful answer based on one weak source remains weak; a short answer with direct records may be strong.
scenes.headline: Fluent wording is not confidence

scenes.narration: Do not invent precise confidence percentages unless the system is actually calibrated for that task. A number like eighty-seven percent can create false precision and hide the real evidence gap.
scenes.headline: Avoid false numeric precision

scenes.narration: Attach each important claim to the evidence that supports it. If the evidence covers only part of the claim, narrow the claim instead of stretching the citation.
scenes.headline: Attach evidence to each claim

scenes.narration: End uncertainty with an action: verify one source, request one missing field, run one check, or stop the high-risk action. The user should know what would resolve the unknown.
scenes.headline: Give every unknown a next action

scenes.narration: Chapter recap. First, separate confirmed facts, inferences, and unknowns.
scenes.headline: 1. Separate claim states

scenes.narration: Second, base confidence on evidence coverage, not fluent wording.
scenes.headline: 2. Follow evidence coverage

scenes.narration: Third, pair every important unknown with a next action.
scenes.headline: 3. Resolve unknowns with action

scenes.narration: Not every sentence deserves the same effort. This chapter shows where the AI Agent must spend verification time before a claim becomes an action.
scenes.headline: Chapter 5: Verify High-Risk Facts
scenes.coreLabel: Spend evidence effort where errors have consequences

scenes.narration: Start with consequence. Verify claims that can move money, change access, affect safety, create legal exposure, damage a reputation, or become difficult to reverse.
scenes.headline: Start with consequence

scenes.narration: Prefer the primary source closest to the fact: the database for account state, the official policy for a rule, the original paper for a result, and the actual artifact for work completion.
scenes.headline: Use the source closest to the fact

scenes.narration: Then seek independent corroboration when one source can be stale, biased, incomplete, or misread. Two pages repeating the same unsupported statement are not two independent sources.
scenes.headline: Corroborate with independent evidence

scenes.narration: Match evidence to the exact claim. A source that proves a product exists does not prove it has a specific feature, and a study in one controlled setting does not prove the same result everywhere.
scenes.headline: Match evidence to the exact claim

scenes.narration: Check date, scope, entity, units, and exceptions. Many confident errors come from a true fact attached to the wrong time, person, market, or condition.
scenes.headline: Check date, scope, entity, and units

scenes.narration: Finally, place a human approval gate before irreversible action. The Agent can collect evidence and propose a decision, but high-impact execution needs explicit authorization.
scenes.headline: Approve irreversible action

scenes.narration: Chapter recap. First, spend verification effort according to consequence.
scenes.headline: 1. Verify by consequence

scenes.narration: Second, use primary evidence and independent corroboration.
scenes.headline: 2. Use primary and independent evidence

scenes.narration: Third, require human approval before irreversible action.
scenes.headline: 3. Gate irreversible action

scenes.narration: Now combine the method into one reusable AI Agent Uncertainty and Fact-Check Checklist you can use before accepting a factual answer.
scenes.headline: Chapter 6: Fact-Check Checklist
scenes.coreLabel: Apply one reusable AI Agent verification tool

scenes.narration: Step one is Answerability. Record the exact question, entity, time range, required freshness, available tools, and the missing detail that would block a reliable answer.
scenes.headline: Step 1: Answerability

scenes.narration: Step two is Claim Inventory. Split the response into consequential claims and label each one confirmed, inferred, or unknown. Ignore harmless style choices and focus on statements that can change a decision.
scenes.headline: Step 2: Claim Inventory

scenes.narration: Step three is Evidence Standard. For each important claim, name the acceptable primary source, the freshness limit, and whether independent corroboration is required.
scenes.headline: Step 3: Evidence Standard

scenes.narration: Step four is Verification. Open the evidence, match it to the claim, check scope and exceptions, and record the result. A citation is useful only when it supports the words you plan to rely on.
scenes.headline: Step 4: Verification

scenes.narration: Step five is Action Risk. Decide whether the result may proceed automatically, needs clarification, requires human approval, or must stop because evidence is missing.
scenes.headline: Step 5: Action Risk

scenes.narration: Step six is Feedback. Save the failed claim, missing tool, or misleading score as a future test so the AI Agent and its evaluation improve together.
scenes.headline: Step 6: Feedback Test

scenes.narration: Chapter recap. First, test answerability and inventory consequential claims.
scenes.headline: 1. Test answerability and claims

scenes.narration: Second, define and verify the evidence standard.
scenes.headline: 2. Define and verify evidence

scenes.narration: Third, connect uncertainty to an action gate and a future test.
scenes.headline: 3. Connect uncertainty to action

scenes.narration: AI Agent hallucinations are not only a mysterious glitch. They arise when uncertain generation meets incentives that reward an answer more than an honest unknown.
scenes.headline: AI Agent guessing is an incentive problem

scenes.narration: First, check whether the question is answerable before judging the response.
scenes.headline: First, test answerability

scenes.narration: Second, separate confirmed facts, inferences, and unknowns.
scenes.headline: Second, separate claim states

scenes.narration: Third, verify high-risk claims with direct evidence and independent corroboration.
scenes.headline: Third, verify high-risk facts

scenes.narration: Use the AI Agent Uncertainty and Fact-Check Checklist to set the evidence bar, choose the action gate, and preserve failures as future tests.
scenes.headline: Use the AI Agent fact-check checklist
scenes.coreLabel: Evidence bar · Action gate · Future test

scenes.narration: The goal is not to make the AI Agent sound less confident. It is to make confidence earn its right to drive a decision.
scenes.headline: Make confidence earn the decision

scenes.narration: Follow Tiny Agent. Tiny Agent helps you get better at using AI.
scenes.headline: Follow Tiny Agent
